A Global Fight for Access to Sanitation in School | UNICEF

Author

Soukaina Belabbes

Published

April 15, 2026

NO SANITATION = NO EDUCATION

Globally, 600 million children do not have a basic sanitation service at school. Where 1 in 5 primary schools have no sanitation service and 1 in 4 primary schools there is no water service. It’s a silent crisis: one that keeps millions of children away from classrooms every single day.

Code 
import subprocess
subprocess.run(['pip', 'install', 'polars', 'plotnine', '-q'], check=True)

import polars as pl
import numpy as np
from plotnine import *
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

indicator_1 = pl.read_csv('/content/unicef_indicator_1 (2) (1).csv')
indicator_2 = pl.read_csv('/content/unicef_indicator_2 (2).csv')
metadata    = pl.read_csv('/content/unicef_metadata (2).csv')

print('✅ Data loaded successfully!')
print(f'  Indicator 1 (child deprivation): {indicator_1.shape[0]} rows')
print(f'  Indicator 2 (school sanitation): {indicator_2.shape[0]} rows')
print(f'  Metadata (country level):        {metadata.shape[0]} rows')
✅ Data loaded successfully!
  Indicator 1 (child deprivation): 216 rows
  Indicator 2 (school sanitation): 2872 rows
  Metadata (country level):        13715 rows
Code 
# We use our custom functions to keep the code clean and avoid repetition
# filter_total_sex() removes sex-disaggregated rows to avoid double counting
# get_most_recent() ensures we always use the latest available data per country

sanitation_recent = (
    filter_total_sex(indicator_2)
    .sort('time_period', descending=True)
    .group_by('country')
    .agg([
        pl.col('obs_value').first().alias('sanitation_pct'),
        pl.col('time_period').first().alias('year'),
        pl.col('alpha_3_code').first(),
    ])
)

deprivation_by_sex = (
    indicator_1
    .filter(pl.col('sex').is_in(['Female', 'Male']))
    .sort('time_period', descending=True)
    .group_by(['country', 'sex'])
    .agg(pl.col('obs_value').first().alias('deprivation_pct'))
)

meta_recent = (
    metadata
    .filter(pl.col('Life expectancy at birth, total (years)').is_not_null())
    .sort('year', descending=True)
    .group_by('country')
    .agg([
        pl.col('GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$)').first().alias('gdp_per_capita'),
        pl.col('Life expectancy at birth, total (years)').first().alias('life_expectancy'),
        pl.col('Population, total').first().alias('population'),
    ])
)

# Join all three datasets on country name
# Left join keeps all countries from sanitation even if metadata is missing
combined = (
    sanitation_recent
    .join(meta_recent, on='country', how='left')
    .filter(pl.col('sanitation_pct').is_not_null())
)

print(f"✅ Sanitation data: {sanitation_recent.shape[0]} countries")
print(f"✅ Deprivation data: {deprivation_by_sex.shape[0]} rows")
print(f"✅ Combined dataset: {combined.shape[0]} countries")

# Quick data quality check using our custom function
print(f"\n📊 Missing values check:")
print(f"  Life expectancy missing: {check_missing(combined, 'life_expectancy')}")
print(f"  GDP missing: {check_missing(combined, 'gdp_per_capita')}")
✅ Sanitation data: 165 countries
✅ Deprivation data: 144 rows
✅ Combined dataset: 165 countries

📊 Missing values check:
  Life expectancy missing: 6
  GDP missing: 12

# ── Exploratory Data Analysis ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
# We explore the data before visualisation to understand its structure,
# check for missing values, and identify key patterns

print("=== INDICATOR 2 : School Sanitation Coverage ===")
print(f"Number of countries: {indicator_2['country'].n_unique()}")
print(f"Years covered: {indicator_2['time_period'].min()} to {indicator_2['time_period'].max()}")
print(f"Average sanitation coverage: {indicator_2['obs_value'].mean():.1f}%")
print(f"Min: {indicator_2['obs_value'].min():.1f}% | Max: {indicator_2['obs_value'].max():.1f}%")
print(f"Missing values: {indicator_2['obs_value'].is_null().sum()}")

print("\n=== INDICATOR 1 : Child Deprivation ===")
print(f"Number of countries: {indicator_1['country'].n_unique()}")
print(f"Average deprivation rate: {indicator_1['obs_value'].mean():.1f}%")
print(f"Missing values: {indicator_1['obs_value'].is_null().sum()}")

print("\n=== METADATA ===")
print(f"Number of countries: {metadata['country'].n_unique()}")
print(f"Years covered: {metadata['year'].min()} to {metadata['year'].max()}")
print(f"Missing life expectancy values: {metadata['Life expectancy at birth, total (years)'].is_null().sum()}")

print("\n=== COMBINED DATASET ===")
print(f"Final dataset: {combined.shape[0]} countries")
print(f"Countries with sanitation below 30%: {combined.filter(pl.col('sanitation_pct') < 30).shape[0]}")
print(f"Countries with sanitation above 90%: {combined.filter(pl.col('sanitation_pct') > 90).shape[0]}")

Code 
# ── Custom functions ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# These reusable functions clean and prepare the data for analysis
# I defined them separately to avoid repeating the same logic multiple times

def get_most_recent(df, value_col, group_col='country'):
    """
    Returns the most recent value per country for a given column.
    We sort by time_period descending so the first value after group_by
    is always the most recent one available.
    """
    return (
        df
        .sort('time_period', descending=True)
        .group_by(group_col)
        .agg(pl.col(value_col).first().alias(value_col))
    )

def filter_total_sex(df):
    """
    Filters the dataframe to keep only rows where sex == 'Total'.
    This avoids double-counting when Female and Male rows are both present.
    """
    return df.filter(pl.col('sex') == 'Total')

def check_missing(df, col):
    """
    Returns the number of missing values in a column.
    Used during EDA to assess data quality before joining datasets.
    """
    return df[col].is_null().sum()

print("✅ Custom functions defined successfully!")
print(f"  - get_most_recent() : extracts latest value per country")
print(f"  - filter_total_sex() : keeps only Total rows")
print(f"  - check_missing()   : counts null values in a column")
✅ Custom functions defined successfully!
  - get_most_recent() : extracts latest value per country
  - filter_total_sex() : keeps only Total rows
  - check_missing()   : counts null values in a column

Where is the crisis hiding ?

Sub-Saharan Africa carries the heaviest burden. Togo (16.8%), Chad (19.4%) and Niger (25.4%) have fewer than 1 in 4 schools with basic sanitation. By contrast, high-income countries report near-universal coverage: revealing a gap that is fundamentally a question of resources and political will, not impossibility.

Code 
# We use plotnine with a world map dataset downloaded from natural earth
# geom_polygon draws each country as a filled polygon coloured by sanitation coverage
import requests, json
import pandas as pd

# Download world map coordinates
url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/datasets/geo-countries/master/data/countries.geojson"
response = requests.get(url)
geo = response.json()

# Extract polygon coordinates into a flat dataframe for plotnine
rows = []
for feature in geo['features']:
    country_name = feature['properties']['name']
    geom = feature['geometry']
    polys = geom['coordinates'] if geom['type'] == 'Polygon' else [p for p in geom['coordinates']]
    for poly_idx, poly in enumerate(polys):
        coords = poly[0] if geom['type'] == 'MultiPolygon' else poly
        for i, (lon, lat) in enumerate(coords):
            rows.append({'country': country_name, 'long': lon, 'lat': lat,
                        'order': i, 'group': f"{country_name}_{poly_idx}"})

world_df = pd.DataFrame(rows)

# Merge with sanitation data
san_pd = sanitation_recent.to_pandas()
world_merged = world_df.merge(san_pd[['country', 'sanitation_pct']],
                               on='country', how='left')

map_plot = (
    ggplot(world_merged, aes(x='long', y='lat', group='group', fill='sanitation_pct'))
    + geom_polygon(color='white', size=0.1)
    + scale_fill_gradient(
        low='#1a5c2a', high='#c8e6c9',
        na_value='#eeeeee',
        name='School Sanitation\nCoverage (%)',
        limits=[0, 100]
    )
    + coord_fixed(ratio=1.3)
    + labs(
        title='School Sanitation Coverage by Country',
        subtitle='Darker green = higher coverage | Light grey = no data available',
        x='', y=''
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        axis_text=element_blank(),
        axis_ticks=element_blank(),
        panel_grid=element_blank(),
        plot_title=element_text(size=13, weight='bold'),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=9, color='grey'),
        legend_position='right'
    )
)
map_plot

The countries with the least also suffer the most AND the gap between girls and boys tells its own story.

Concerns are especially concentrated in Niger, where the deprivation rate is 35% for girls, which is more than four times the global average. In countries where education is legally required, poverty and missing infrastructure make it impossible for millions to comply.

Code 
# Select top 12 countries by female deprivation rate
top_countries_dep = (
    deprivation_by_sex
    .filter(pl.col('sex') == 'Female')
    .sort('deprivation_pct', descending=True)
    .head(12)
    .select('country')
)

dep_bar_df = (
    deprivation_by_sex
    .join(top_countries_dep, on='country', how='inner')
    .to_pandas()
)

# Order countries by female deprivation for clean display
female_order = (
    dep_bar_df[dep_bar_df['sex'] == 'Female']
    .sort_values('deprivation_pct', ascending=True)['country'].tolist()
)
dep_bar_df['country'] = dep_bar_df['country'].astype('category').cat.set_categories(female_order)

bar_plot = (
    ggplot(dep_bar_df, aes(x='country', y='deprivation_pct', fill='sex'))
    + geom_col(position='dodge', width=0.7)
    + scale_fill_manual(values={'Female': '#2e7d32', 'Male': '#81c784'}, name='Sex')
    + coord_flip()
    + labs(
        title='Severe Child Deprivation: Girls vs Boys',
        subtitle='% of children suffering two or more deprivations — top 12 most affected countries',
        x='',
        y='% Children with Severe Deprivation'
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        plot_title=element_text(size=13, weight='bold'),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=9, color='grey'),
        axis_text_y=element_text(size=10),
        panel_grid_major_y=element_blank(),
        legend_position='top'
    )
)
bar_plot

Clean schools, longer lives

The correlation between school sanitation and life expectancy is strongly significant (r = 0.71). Every country with sanitation below 30% also has a life expectancy under 70 years.

This is not coincidence. It is the compound effect of poor health, higher absenteeism, and weaker education outcomes across entire generations.

Code 
scatter_df = (
    combined
    .filter(
        pl.col('life_expectancy').is_not_null() &
        pl.col('sanitation_pct').is_not_null()
    )
    .to_pandas()
)

scatter_plot = (
    ggplot(scatter_df, aes(x='sanitation_pct', y='life_expectancy'))
    + geom_point(alpha=0.6, color='#2e7d32', size=3)
    + geom_smooth(method='lm', color='black', se=False, size=1.2)
    + labs(
        title='Sanitation vs Life Expectancy',
        subtitle='Each dot = one country | Black line = linear regression (r = 0.71)',
        x='School Sanitation Coverage (%)',
        y='Life Expectancy at Birth (years)'
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        plot_title=element_text(size=13, weight='bold'),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=9, color='grey'),
    )
)
scatter_plot

A Crisis, 23 Years In The Making

The global average dropped 19 points between 2000 and 2013: population growth outpacing infrastructure. The 2015 SDGs brought renewed focus. But in 2023, coverage is still 14 points below the year 2000.

Progress exists. But it is not fast enough.

Code 
global_trend = (
    indicator_2
    .filter(pl.col('sex') == 'Total')
    .group_by('time_period')
    .agg(pl.col('obs_value').mean().alias('avg_sanitation'))
    .sort('time_period')
    .to_pandas()
)
global_trend['country'] = 'Global Average'

focus_countries = ['Chad', 'Niger', 'Togo', 'Ethiopia', 'Ghana', 'Bangladesh']
country_trends = (
    indicator_2
    .filter(
        (pl.col('sex') == 'Total') &
        pl.col('country').is_in(focus_countries)
    )
    .select(['country', 'time_period', 'obs_value'])
    .rename({'obs_value': 'avg_sanitation'})
    .to_pandas()
)

ts_plot = (
    ggplot()
    + geom_line(
        data=country_trends,
        mapping=aes(x='time_period', y='avg_sanitation', color='country'),
        size=1.0, alpha=0.85
    )
    + geom_line(
        data=global_trend,
        mapping=aes(x='time_period', y='avg_sanitation'),
        size=1.5, color='black', linetype='dashed'
    )
    + annotate('text', x=2002, y=global_trend['avg_sanitation'].mean() + 5,
               label='Global average', size=8, color='black')
    + scale_color_brewer(type='qual', palette='Set2', name='Country')
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2000, 2024, 4)))
    + labs(
        title='Evolution of Sanitation (2000–2023)',
        subtitle='Dashed black = global average | Coloured lines = selected low-access countries',
        x='Time Period',
        y='School Sanitation Coverage (%)'
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        plot_title=element_text(size=13, weight='bold'),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=9, color='grey'),
        legend_position='right'
    )
)
ts_plot

What happens without sanitation?

The consequences of missing school sanitation fall into four interconnected crises:

  • 🚫 Girls drop out — Without private facilities, adolescent girls cannot manage menstruation at school — and many simply stay home instead.
  • 🦠 Disease spreads — No handwashing means preventable illnesses circulate freely. Sick children miss class, fall behind, and eventually leave school.
  • 📚 Learning collapses — Schools without sanitation see higher absenteeism, lower literacy outcomes, and reduced teacher retention over time.
  • 💔 Dignity denied — A child who cannot use a safe toilet at school is a child whose basic dignity is not being protected by their society.

Conclusion

Despite progress, far too many children are still left behind. In 2000, 96.1% of schools worldwide had basic sanitation. In 2023, that figure stands at 82.5%. The first and most crucial step is raising awareness. By working together, we can ensure that every child (regardless of country, income, or background) learns in a clean, safe school.

By Soukaina Belabbes
Sources: UNICEF, WHO/UNICEF Joint Monitoring Programme (JMP) for Water Supply, Sanitation and Hygiene, 2023
